elements.sent-2411-072: We overview these techniques in the following subsection.
elements.sent-2411-073: We first describe, at a high level, a sequential algorithm for random walk sampling, which will serve as our starting point.
elements.sent-2411-074: We will then describe how to adapt this sequential algorithm to the \cc{ model.}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, several challenges arise while porting the sequential algorithm to the distributed setting.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the remainder of the section, we discuss these challenges and the key ideas that we use to address them.. Sequential algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-077: Consider the following sequential algorithm that constructs a random walk across multiple phases.
elements.sent-2411-078: In each phase, a new segment of the walk is sampled and this process is repeated until the full walk is constructed.
elements.sent-2411-079: Within each phase we employ two key ideas that make the algorithm more amenable to efficient implementation in the \cc{ model: (i) the walk segment constructed in each phase contains $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ distinct vertices and (ii) the algorithm constructs the relevant walk segment by filling in the walk using a top down approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In short, given a starting vertex and an ending vertex of a walk, a midpoint is sampled (conditioned on the two endpoints) and then we recurse on the two halves.
elements.sent-2411-081: We give more details on this filling in process in later paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-082: As we will see shortly, these two ideas allow us to effectively share computation in parallel across multiple machines in the \cc{ model.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We need one additional idea to ensure that the algorithm is efficient.
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that since we are only interested in the first entry edges of the random walk into each vertex, we can skip over vertices visited in previous phases.
elements.sent-2411-085: In order to do this ``short cutting'' over previously visited vertices, we work with the Schur complement graph \cite{Kyng_2017, shortcutAS , combined with the short-cutting technique from Kelner and Mądry \cite{MK .. Distributed algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Given the above outline of a sequential sampling algorithm, let us focus on transforming it into a distributed algorithm in the \cc\ model.
elements.sent-2411-087: Suppose that a leader machine $\sfM$ is responsible for maintaining the walk segment constructed in a phase.
elements.sent-2411-088: Further, suppose that just before an iteration of the algorithm, which we refer to as a level, $\sfM$ holds a ``partial walk'' with $O(\sqrt{n )$ distinct vertices and its goal is to insert a midpoint between every consecutive pair of vertices in the walk.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Since we are filling in the walk in a top-down manner, by a ``partial walk'' we mean a sequence of vertices in which each pair of consecutive vertices are the end points of an as-yet-unknown random walk of length $\delta > 1$ in the graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Once midpoints are inserted between consecutive vertices in this partial walk, we get a partial walk that has twice as many vertices, with the consecutive vertices being the end points of an as-yet-unknown length $\delta/2$ random walk in the graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-091: The midpoints are sampled using pre-computed powers of the transition matrix $\adj$ of $G$; specifically, the midpoints at this level are sampled using $\adj^{\delta/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: These matrix powers are computed using the fast \cc{ matrix multiplication algorithm in \cite{MatrixMult .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The fact that the partial walk contains $O(\sqrt{n )$ distinct vertices (even though it can contain many more vertices) implies that there are $O(n)$ distinct start-end pairs into which midpoints need to be inserted.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Thus, each start-end pair can be assigned to a distinct machine.
elements.sent-2411-095: However, each start-end pair can appear multiple times in the partial walk and the machine assigned to a particular start-end pair is responsible for generating not just one, but multiple midpoints that then need to be sent back to the leader machine $\sfM$ to be stitched into the partial walk.
elements.sent-2411-096: These high-level ideas are illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:midPointPlacement .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.95\textwidth]{MidPoint.png} \caption{This figure shows the high level process of machine \sfM{} adding midpoints to the walk $W_i$ to obtain $W_{i+1}$. Note that in $W_i$ there exist the distinct start-end pairs: $(1,3),(3,2),(2,1),(1,2)$. \sfM{} sends the count of each start-end pair to the machine responsible for that pair. The machines responsible for each start-end pair then generate a sequence containing the appropriate number of midpoints and then collectively send the multiset of generated midpoints to \sfM. The final midpoint, in green, is sent separately from the multiset and is always placed in the final position. The * indicates that $\sfM_{1,3}$ is responsible for the final midpoint. Finally, \sfM{} samples a perfect matching between the sampled midpoints and spaces in the walk, shown with red arrows. The midpoints are then placed in the walk in these selected indices. This figure ignores the subtlety of ensuring that $W_{i+1}$ contains at most $O(n)$ distinct vertices.} \label{fig:midPointPlacement}
elements.sent-2411-097: During this process we face two major challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-098: The algorithm needs to maintain the invariant that the partial walk cannot contain more than $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ distinct vertices at the end of the level.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The machine responsible for each start-end pair cannot communicate to machine $\sfM$ specific indices (or positions) of the midpoints it generates.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is because even though the partial walk contains only $O(n)$ distinct start-end pairs, a start-end pair may appear as many as $O(n^3)$ times in the partial walk.
elements.sent-2411-102: To address the first obstacle, our algorithm ensures that the leader machine $\sfM$ only receives the midpoints up to some truncation point of its partial walk such that the number of distinct vertices within the prefix of the partial walk (up to the truncation point) do not exceed $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: This seems daunting at first because $\sfM$ has to determine a truncation point for the walk without even looking at it.
elements.sent-2411-104: We solve this issue by using distributed binary search.
elements.sent-2411-105: Specifically, $\sfM$ guesses a truncation point and distributes requests for midpoint generation for start-end pairs in the partial walk up to the truncation point.
elements.sent-2411-106: We show how machines receiving midpoint generation requests can aggregate their answers efficiently to determine if midpoint generation up to the current truncation point will cause the $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ budget on the number of distinct vertices in the partial walk to be exceeded.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Accordingly $\sfM$ can adjust its truncation point guess and this binary search process continues until $\sfM$ finds the largest truncation point such that the new partial walk up to that truncation point contains $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ distinct vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Now, we focus on the second challenge.
elements.sent-2411-110: Once generated, the combined information regarding the identity of the midpoints and their ordering along the walk is prohibitively large to be communicated back to leader machine $\sfM$, so we need to compress this data in a way such that $\sfM$ will be able to resample the walk locally.
elements.sent-2411-111: Towards this we use the following key ideas: first, $\sfM$ only collects a multiset of midpoints, without any information about their position in the walk and secondly, we use a perfect matching sampling algorithm to resample the locations of the midpoints.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is clear that the first idea allows us to save on communication bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-113: Surprisingly, the compressed information (as multisets) is also sufficient for resampling.
elements.sent-2411-114: Since this is a nontrivial observation, we further expand upon how $\sfM$ executes the placement process of collected midpoints.
elements.sent-2411-115: Machine $\sfM$ locally constructs a complete bipartite graph, where vertices on one side of the bipartition corresponds to the sampled midpoints and vertices on the other side correspond to positions, identified by start-end pairs, in the walk.
elements.sent-2411-116: We then assign to each edge a weight that corresponds to the probability of that sampled midpoint appearing as a midpoint for that start-end pair and define the weight of a perfect matching as the product of all of its edge weights.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our algorithm then assigns the midpoints to positions in the walk by sampling a random perfect matching with probability proportional to its weight.
elements.sent-2411-118: We show that the leader machine $\sfM$ can do this sampling approximately in polynomial time by appealing to the classical results of Jerrum, Sinclair, and Vigoda on approximating the permanent \cite{Permanent and Jerrum, Valiant, and Vazirani \cite{JVV on reducing sampling to counting.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Using Schur complement and short cut graphs.
elements.sent-2411-120: The final piece of our algorithm is the short-cutting process.
elements.sent-2411-121: Recall that the idea is to skip over vertices in each phase that were visited in prior phases.
elements.sent-2411-122: It is evident that short-cutting applies only after the first phase.
elements.sent-2411-123: We use two types of graphs for this -- the Schur complement graph and shortcut graph.
elements.sent-2411-124: Consider the situation just before a phase and let $S$ be the set of vertices in the input graph $G$ that have not yet been visited along with the last vertex visited in the previous phase.
elements.sent-2411-125: The Schur complement of $G$, denoted by $\schur(G,S)$, is an edge-weighted, undirected graph with vertex set $S$ such that, roughly speaking, taking a random walk on $\schur(G,S)$ yields the same distribution as taking a random walk on $G$ and then removing the vertices in $V \setminus S$.
elements.sent-2411-126: In essence, $\schur(G,S)$ allows us to take a random walk on $G$ while skipping over vertices in $V \setminus S$.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, the walk on $\schur(G, S)$ returns edges from $\schur(G, S)$.
elements.sent-2411-128: For our spanning tree sampling, we need the first visit edges in the original graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-129: For this, using ideas from~\cite{MK , we need another derivative graph on $G$, that we call the shortcut graph.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Interestingly, we show that using fast distributed matrix multiplication algorithms, both of these graphs can be computed efficiently in each phase.

elements.sent-2411-145: In this section we describe all the relevant notations and setup that will be used in subsequent sections.
elements.sent-2411-147: We use $G$ to denote the input graph to our algorithm and following convention, $n$ and $m$ will denote the number of vertices and edges in $G$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-148: We use $\adj$ to denote the transition matrix of the random walk on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-149: In particular, any vertex has equal probability of transitioning to any of its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-150: Given an $n$-vertex input graph $G = (V, E)$, the underlying communication network is the size-$n$ clique $K_n$, along with a bijection between vertices in $G$ and the machines in the communication network.
elements.sent-2411-151: Thus each machine hosts a distinct vertex and all edges in $G$ incident to that vertex.
elements.sent-2411-152: Machines have unique IDs of length $O(\log n)$ bits and without loss of generality for our algorithms, we say the machines have IDs $1$ through $n$ and the machine with ID $i$ holds vertex $i$.
elements.sent-2411-153: Computation in the model proceeds in synchronous rounds.
elements.sent-2411-154: Each round begins with each machine performing unbounded local computation.
elements.sent-2411-155: A round then ends with each machine sending a (possibly different) message of length $O(\log n)$ bits to every other machine.
elements.sent-2411-156: Note that a single message is able to encode a constant number of vertices or edges.
elements.sent-2411-157: The time complexity of an algorithm is then measured by the number of rounds used.
elements.sent-2411-158: While machines are theoretically allotted unlimited time for local computation, it is preferred that this time is polynomial in terms of the input graph size.
elements.sent-2411-159: Our algorithms only require polynomial time local computation.
elements.sent-2411-160: Lenzen \cite{Lenzen13 proved that in $O(1)$ deterministic rounds it is possible for every vertex to send and receive a total of $O(n)$ messages, regardless of the specific destination of each message.}

elements.sent-2411-174: The motivation for defining $\schur(G, S)$ is that taking a random walk in $G$ starting at $v \in S$ and only looking at the subsequence of visits to vertices in $S$ is identical to taking a random walk in \schur$(G,S)$ starting from the vertex $v \in S$.
elements.sent-2411-175: More precisely, as shown in Theorem 2.4 in \cite{shortcutAS , the distributions over the sequences of vertices in $S$ generated by the two random walks are identical\footnote{Here a random walk on a weighted undirected or directed graph refers to choosing an outgoing edge to traverse in each transition proportional to its weight.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is also possible to define the graph $\schur(G, S)$ implicitly (and more intuitively) by specifying the transition matrix, $\scc$, of a random walk on the graph.
elements.sent-2411-177: Specifically, for any $u, v \in S$, $\scc[u,v]$ gives the probability that $v$ is the first vertex in $S \setminus \{u\}$ that a random walk started at $u$ in $G$ visits.

elements.sent-2411-427: In this section we present a ``load balanced'' version of the \dbling algorithm that can compute a length-$n$ walk in $\pln$ rounds in the \cc{ model.. High-level idea.}
elements.sent-2411-428: To compute a length-$\tau$ random walk, the \dbling algorithm starts with each vertex $v$ computing $\tau$ length-1 random walks (i.e., random edges) originating at $v$.
elements.sent-2411-429: At each iteration, walks ending at a node $v$ are merged with walks originating at $v$.
elements.sent-2411-430: This halves the number of walks held by a vertex, while doubling the length of every walk.
elements.sent-2411-431: After $O(\log \tau)$ of these ``doubling'' iterations, every node $v$ holds a length-$\tau$ random walk originating at vertex $v$.
elements.sent-2411-432: While each walk is a proper random walk, because of how the walks are merged (described in detail below), walks originating at different vertices are not independent.
elements.sent-2411-433: The main challenge for the \dbling algorithm is to implement the merging step efficiently in the \cc{ model.}
elements.sent-2411-434: It turns out that even for relatively short walks, i.e., with $\tau = \Theta(n)$, a faithful implementation of the \dbling algorithm requires $\Omega(n^2 \log n)$ bits to travel to a particular vertex in a single merging step in the worst case, which requires $\Omega(n)$ rounds in the \cc{ model.}
elements.sent-2411-435: We add a ``load balancing'' component to each merging step so as to take advantage of the overall $\Theta(n^2)$ bandwidth of the \cc{ model.}
elements.sent-2411-436: We show that with this ``load balancing'' step in place, each merging step takes $O(\log n)$ rounds, w.h.p.. Load-balanced Doubling Algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-480: Sampling random spanning trees uniformly is a fundamental problem with beautiful mathematical connections to random walks, electrical circuits, and graph Laplacians.
elements.sent-2411-481: The problem also has important applications to graph sparsifiers and serves as the basis for new approximation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-482: Using the Aldous-Broder algorithm based on random walks, we present the first sublinear round algorithm for approximately sampling uniform spanning trees in the \cc{ model of distributed computing.}
elements.sent-2411-483: In particular, our algorithm requires $\Tilde{O (n^{1/2 + \alpha )$ rounds for sampling a spanning tree from a distribution with total variation distance $1/n^c$, for arbitrary constant $c > 0$, from the uniform distribution.}}

elements.subsection-2411-013: Overview of Our Algorithm and Key Techniques
elements.sent-2411-557: We first describe a very simple, sequential, recursive algorithm for generating a random walk; this will be a top-down algorithm and serve as our starting point.
elements.sent-2411-558: By choosing the length $\ell$ of the walk to be $\Tilde{\Theta (n^3)$, by the result of Aldous and Broder \cite{AAB, BAB , we also obtain a random spanning tree with high probability.}}

elements.sent-2411-561: In our final attempt, we describe, at a high level, the first $o(n)$-round algorithm for sampling random spanning trees.
elements.sent-2411-562: For all of these algorithms we will choose $\ell$ to be a power of two for convenience.
elements.sent-2411-563: Consider the following recursive algorithm that samples a uniformly distributed random walk of length $\ell$ starting at an input vertex, $s$.
elements.sent-2411-564: The algorithm first samples an end vertex $e$ of an $\ell$-length random walk that starts at $s$ and then feeds $s,e$ and $\ell$ to a recursive subroutine, that we call Fill.
elements.sent-2411-565: The subroutine Fill then samples a midpoint vertex $m$ of an $\ell$-length random walk that starts at $s$ and ends at $e$, using Bayes' rule.
elements.sent-2411-566: More precisely, we want to sample the midpoint of an $\ell$ length random walk beginning at $s$ and conditioned on ending at $e$.
elements.sent-2411-567: By the Markov property of random walks, the probability that vertex $v$ is the midpoint of such a walk is proportional to the probability that an $\frac{\ell {2}$ length walk starting at $s$ ends at $v$ times the probability that another $\frac{\ell {2}$ length walk starting at $v$ ends at $e$.}}
elements.sent-2411-568: Then the subroutine recursively generates an $\ell/2$-length walk with $(s,m)$ as start-end vertices and another $\ell/2$-length walk with $(m,e)$ as start-end vertices.
elements.sent-2411-569: Finally, it joins the two $\ell/2$-length walks and returns the resulting $\ell$-length walk.
elements.sent-2411-570: Note that all of the vertex sampling during an entire run of the algorithm can be done using $O(\log \ell)$ distinct powers of the transition matrix, $\adj$, of the input graph.
elements.sent-2411-571: This includes the endpoint $e$ and all of the midpoints.
elements.sent-2411-572: We compute all of these matrices in advance, before entering the recursion.

elements.sent-2411-581: We now focus on transforming the above outline of a sequential random walk sampling algorithm into a distributed algorithm in the \cc\ model.
elements.sent-2411-582: We begin by making a natural, structural shift -- in each level of the recursion all of the work will be done in parallel across multiple machines.
elements.sent-2411-583: Recall that during each level of the recursive sequential algorithm a set of midpoints is generated and inserted into the walk.
elements.sent-2411-584: Our goal is to delegate this midpoint generation process to multiple machines and then to centrally collect the generated midpoints and insert them into their respective correct locations.
elements.sent-2411-585: In our notation, we let $W$ be the walk under construction.

elements.sent-2411-587: We let $W[j]$ refer to the vertex at index $j$ of $W$; this indexing takes into account the yet-to-be-filled holes.
elements.sent-2411-588: We let $W_i$ refer to the partial walk that has been constructed before level $i$ of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-589: When we refer to adjacent vertices in $W_i$, we mean vertices that are only separated by holes.
elements.sent-2411-590: We assume that we are given a start vertex $s$ and designate the machine $\sfM$ holding $s$ as the leader machine.
elements.sent-2411-591: We initialize the walk with its first vertex, i.e., set $W[0] = s$.
elements.sent-2411-592: During the course of the algorithm, $\sfM$ is the machine that will store the random walk as it is constructed.
elements.sent-2411-593: We then compute the relevant powers of the transition matrix $\adj$ and sample the end point $e$ using the row $s$ of the matrix $\adj^{\ell}$.
elements.sent-2411-594: We insert $e$ into $W$, as its last vertex, i.e., we set $W[\ell] = e$.
elements.sent-2411-595: We are now ready to fill in the rest of the walk level-by-level by sampling midpoints.
elements.sent-2411-596: Now assume that the algorithm is just about to enter the $i$-th level.
elements.sent-2411-597: At this time, $\sfM$ holds $W_i$, a partial walk with $2^{i-1 +1$ evenly spaced vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-598: Note that the ``gap'' between consecutive filled in vertices in $W_i$ is $\frac{\ell {2^{i-1 }$.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: [H] \caption{Midpoint Requests and Generation} \label{markov-alg-main} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Machine $\sfM$ holds the current partial walk $W_i$. \State $\sfM$ counts the number of distinct consecutive pairs in $W_i$ and the number of occurrences $c_{p,q}$ of each pair $(p, q)$. \State $\sfM$ assigns one machine, $\sfM_{p,q}$, to each (start, end) pair $(p, q)$ in $W_i$ and sends $\sfM_{p, q}$ the corresponding count $c_{p, q}$. \State $\sfM_{p, q}$ requests and receives the probability $\adj^{\delta/2}[p,j]\cdot \adj^{\delta/2}[j, q]$ from each machine $j$ hosting vertex $j$. \State Machine $\sfM_{p,q}$ samples the midpoint sequence $\Pi_{p,q} = \pi_1, \ldots ,\pi_{c_{p, q}}$, where each $\pi_k$ is sampled according to the (unnormalized) distribution $\left(\adj^{\delta/2}[p,j]\cdot \adj^{\delta/2}[j, q]\right)_{j=1}^n$ that machine $\sfM_{p,q}$ received. \end{algorithmic}
